keyPoints: **This Week:** Open retirement account (Roth IRA or sign up for 401k)

title: Introduction
content: Taxes don't have to be scary or confusing. Understanding the basics empowers you to keep more of your hard-earned money, avoid costly mistakes, and make smarter financial decisions year-round. This guide breaks down everything you need to know.

title: It's Your Largest Expense:
content:
- Federal income tax
- State income tax (most states)
- FICA (Social Security & Medicare): 7.65%
- Sales tax
- Property tax
- Can total 25-40% of your income!

title: You Can Reduce It Legally:
content:
- Deductions lower taxable income
- Credits reduce tax owed dollar-for-dollar
- Strategic decisions save thousands
- Knowledge = Money in your pocket

title: Avoid Costly Mistakes:
content:
- Missing deductions/credits
- Paying penalties
- Audit risks
- Interest on underpayment

title: How Tax Brackets Work:
content:
**Common Myth:** "If I earn more and jump to a higher bracket, I'll actually take home less!"
**TRUTH:** Only the dollars ABOVE each threshold are taxed at the higher rate. You never lose money by earning more.

title: Example: $50,000 Income
content:
- First $11,600 taxed at 10% = $1,160
- Next $35,550 taxed at 12% = $4,266
- Remaining $2,850 taxed at 22% = $627
- **Total federal tax: $6,053** (12.1% effective rate, not 22%!)

title: Your Effective Rate vs. Marginal Rate:
content:
- **Marginal rate:** Highest bracket you're in (22% in example)
- **Effective rate:** Actual percentage paid (12.1% in example)
- Always lower than marginal rate
- What actually matters for planning
keyPoints: **Marginal rate:** Highest bracket you're in (22% in example)
**Effective rate:** Actual percentage paid (12.1% in example)

title: Income Tax (Federal & State):
content:
- Tax on wages, salaries, tips
- Self-employment income
- Investment income
- Rental income
- Most states have it (7 don't: AK, FL, NV, SD, TN, TX, WY)
keyPoints: Most states have it (7 don't: AK, FL, NV, SD, TN, TX, WY)

title: FICA (Payroll Taxes):
content:
- Social Security: 6.2% (employer matches)
- Medicare: 1.45% (employer matches)
- Total: 7.65% from your paycheck
- Self-employed pay both halves (15.3%)

title: Capital Gains Tax:
content:
- Tax on profit from selling investments
- Short-term (under 1 year): Ordinary income rate
- Long-term (over 1 year): 0%, 15%, or 20% depending on income
- Huge incentive to hold investments longer
keyPoints: Tax on profit from selling investments
Long-term (over 1 year): 0%, 15%, or 20% depending on income
Huge incentive to hold investments longer

title: Property Tax:
content:
- Tax on home/land value
- Varies widely by location
- Usually 1-2% of home value annually
- Often deductible (up to $10,000)

title: Sales Tax:
content:
- Tax on purchases
- State and local (0-10%+)
- Can't avoid except in states without sales tax
keyPoints: Can't avoid except in states without sales tax

title: Standard Deduction (2024):
content:
- Single: $14,600
- Married Filing Jointly: $29,200
- Head of Household: $21,900
- Reduces taxable income automatically
- No tracking required
- Most people use this (90%+)

title: Itemized Deductions:
content:
- Add up individual deductions
- Only use if total exceeds standard deduction
- Requires tracking and records
- Common itemized deductions:
  - Mortgage interest
  - State/local taxes (SALT) up to $10,000
  - Charitable contributions
  - Medical expenses (over 7.5% of AGI)
  - Casualty losses (in federal disaster areas)
keyPoints: Only use if total exceeds standard deduction

title: Should You Itemize?
content:
- Do the math
- Most people don't have enough to beat standard deduction
- Homeowners might
- High charitable giving might
- High state taxes might
keyPoints: Most people don't have enough to beat standard deduction

title: Deduction:
content:
- Reduces taxable income
- Value depends on tax bracket
- $1,000 deduction in 22% bracket = $220 tax savings

title: Credit:
content:
- Reduces tax owed dollar-for-dollar
- $1,000 credit = $1,000 tax savings
- Much more valuable!
- Two types:
  - **Refundable:** Get money back even if you owe $0 tax
  - **Non-refundable:** Only reduces tax to $0, no refund
keyPoints: **Refundable:** Get money back even if you owe $0 tax
**Non-refundable:** Only reduces tax to $0, no refund

title: Child Tax Credit:
content:
- $2,000 per child under 17
- Partially refundable ($1,600)
- Income limits apply
- Massive benefit for families

title: Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC):
content:
- For low-to-moderate income workers
- Refundable (can get money back)
- $600-$7,430 depending on income and children
- Many eligible people don't claim it!
keyPoints: Many eligible people don't claim it!

title: Child and Dependent Care Credit:
content:
- For childcare expenses while you work
- Up to $3,000 per child ($6,000 for 2+)
- 20-35% of expenses
- Helps working parents
keyPoints: For childcare expenses while you work

title: American Opportunity Credit:
content:
- Up to $2,500 per student
- First 4 years of college
- Partially refundable
- Covers tuition, fees, books

title: Lifetime Learning Credit:
content:
- Up to $2,000 per tax return
- Any post-secondary education
- Not refundable
- Income limits apply

title: Saver's Credit:
content:
- For retirement contributions (IRA, 401k)
- 10%, 20%, or 50% of contribution
- Up to $2,000 ($4,000 married)
- For low-to-moderate income
- Encourages retirement saving

title: Pre-Tax Contributions (Traditional 401k/IRA):
content:
- Deduct contribution from taxable income
- $5,000 contribution in 22% bracket = $1,100 tax savings NOW
- Pay taxes when you withdraw in retirement
- Good if you expect lower tax rate in retirement
keyPoints: $5,000 contribution in 22% bracket = $1,100 tax savings NOW
Pay taxes when you withdraw in retirement
Good if you expect lower tax rate in retirement

title: Roth Contributions (Roth 401k/IRA):
content:
- No deduction now
- Withdrawals tax-free in retirement
- Good if you expect higher tax rate in retirement
- Especially good for young workers
keyPoints: Good if you expect higher tax rate in retirement

title: Example:
content:
- Contribute $6,000 to Traditional IRA
- 22% tax bracket
- Reduces current tax bill by $1,320
- Money grows tax-deferred

title: Introduction
content:
If you work for a school, hospital, church, or non-profit organization, you likely have access to a 403(b) retirement plan instead of a traditional 401(k). Understanding how it works helps you maximize this valuable benefit.
## What is a 403(b)?
A 403(b) is a tax-advantaged retirement plan for employees of:
- Public schools and universities
- Hospitals and healthcare systems
- Churches and religious organizations
- 501(c)(3) non-profit organizations
- Government research organizations
**Named after the IRS code section**, it works similarly to a 401(k) but with some unique features and rules.
## 403(b) vs 401(k): Key Differences

title: Who Can Participate:
content:
- **403(b)**: Teachers, nurses, librarians, clergy, non-profit employees
- **401(k)**: For-profit company employees
keyPoints: **403(b)**: Teachers, nurses, librarians, clergy, non-profit employees

title: Investment Options:
content:
- **403(b)**: Traditionally annuities and mutual funds
- **401(k)**: Wider range of investment options

title: Employer Match:
content:
- **403(b)**: Less common (but some offer)
- **401(k)**: More common

title: Special Catch-Up:
content:
- **403(b)**: Can have 15-year catch-up provision
- **401(k)**: Only age 50+ catch-up

title: Similarities:
content:
- Same contribution limits
- Same tax treatment (pre-tax or Roth)
- Same early withdrawal penalties
- Same required minimum distributions
## Contribution Limits (2024)

title: Regular Contributions:
content:
- Under 50: $23,000/year
- Age 50+: $30,500/year (extra $7,500 catch-up)

title: Annuity option:
content:
- Immediate annuity: $2,100/month for life
- $25,200/year
- Nothing left for heirs
- No inflation adjustment

title: 15-Year Rule (Special to 403(b)):
content:
If you've worked for the same employer for 15+ years:
- Extra $3,000/year catch-up
- Lifetime maximum of $15,000
- Can use IN ADDITION to age 50+ catch-up
**Example:** Teacher age 52 with 20 years at same school:
- Base: $23,000
- Age 50+ catch-up: $7,500
- 15-year rule: $3,000
- **Total: $33,500/year**
## Traditional vs Roth 403(b)
keyPoints: Can use IN ADDITION to age 50+ catch-up

title: Traditional 403(b):
content:
- Contributions are PRE-TAX
- Reduces taxable income now
- Pay taxes when you withdraw in retirement
- Best if: Higher tax bracket now than retirement
keyPoints: Pay taxes when you withdraw in retirement
Best if: Higher tax bracket now than retirement

title: Roth 403(b):
content:
- Contributions are AFTER-TAX
- No tax deduction now
- Withdrawals are TAX-FREE in retirement
- Best if: Lower tax bracket now, expect higher in retirement
- Great for young teachers starting career
**You can split contributions** between Traditional and Roth!
- Example: 70% Traditional, 30% Roth
- Provides tax diversification in retirement
## Investment Options
keyPoints: Best if: Lower tax bracket now, expect higher in retirement
Great for young teachers starting career

title: Annuities:
content:
Many 403(b) plans traditionally offered annuities:
- Fixed annuities (guaranteed return)
- Variable annuities (market-based)
- Often have higher fees
- Some have surrender charges

title: Mutual Funds:
content:
Most modern 403(b)s now offer mutual funds:
- Target-date funds (easiest choice)
- Index funds (low-cost, diversified)
- Actively managed funds
- Similar to 401(k) options

title: What to Choose:
content:
- **Best for most**: Low-cost index funds or target-date funds
- **Avoid**: High-fee annuities (unless very low cost)
- **Watch for**: Expense ratios over 0.5%
## Employer Match
While less common than 401(k) matching, some 403(b) plans offer:
- Typical match: 3-6% of salary
- Vesting schedules (you own match after X years)
- Some non-profits contribute even without employee contribution
keyPoints: **Best for most**: Low-cost index funds or target-date funds
Vesting schedules (you own match after X years)

title: If your employer offers matching:
content:
- Contribute AT LEAST enough to get full match
- This is free money (instant 100% return)
- Match is always Traditional, even if you choose Roth
## Who Should Use a 403(b)?
keyPoints: Contribute AT LEAST enough to get full match
Match is always Traditional, even if you choose Roth

title: Definitely max out if:
content:
- ✓ Your employer offers matching
- ✓ You have no high-interest debt
- ✓ You have emergency savings
- ✓ Investment options are low-cost

title: Contribution priority:
content:
1. **Contribute to get full employer match**
2. **Max out Roth IRA** ($7,000/year in 2024)
3. **Return to 403(b)** - contribute up to limit
4. **Taxable brokerage** if still have money to invest
## Common 403(b) Mistakes
keyPoints: **Taxable brokerage** if still have money to invest

title: Mistake 1: Not Participating
content:
- Missing employer match
- Delaying retirement savings
- **Solution**: Start with at least match amount
keyPoints: **Solution**: Start with at least match amount

title: Mistake 2: Choosing High-Fee Annuities
content:
- Fees of 2-3% can cost hundreds of thousands over career
- **Solution**: Choose low-cost index funds if available
keyPoints: Fees of 2-3% can cost hundreds of thousands over career
**Solution**: Choose low-cost index funds if available

title: Mistake 3: Not Increasing Contributions
content:
- Starting at 3% and never raising
- **Solution**: Increase 1% each year or with each raise
keyPoints: **Solution**: Increase 1% each year or with each raise

title: Mistake 4: Cashing Out When Changing Jobs
content:
- Triggers taxes and penalties
- **Solution**: Roll over to new employer's plan or IRA
keyPoints: **Solution**: Roll over to new employer's plan or IRA

title: Mistake 5: Forgetting About Old 403(b)s
content:
- Leaving accounts at old employers
- Losing track of money
- **Solution**: Consolidate to current employer or IRA
## Rolling Over Your 403(b)
keyPoints: **Solution**: Consolidate to current employer or IRA

title: Option 1: Leave it
content:
- Pro: No action needed, stays invested
- Con: Can't contribute more, fees may be high
keyPoints: Pro: No action needed, stays invested
Con: Can't contribute more, fees may be high

title: Option 2: Roll to new employer's plan
content:
- Pro: Consolidation, may have better options
- Con: Limited to new employer's investments
keyPoints: Pro: Consolidation, may have better options
Con: Limited to new employer's investments

title: Option 3: Roll to IRA
content:
- Pro: Unlimited investment choices, lower fees
- Pro: More control and flexibility
- Con: May lose 15-year catch-up provision
- **Best for most people**
keyPoints: Pro: Unlimited investment choices, lower fees

title: Option 4: Cash out (DON'T DO THIS)
content:
- 10% penalty if under 59½
- Income taxes on entire amount
- Lose decades of compound growth

title: How to roll over:
content:
1. Open IRA with low-cost provider (Vanguard, Fidelity, Schwab)
2. Request **direct rollover** from 403(b) to IRA
3. Never touch the money yourself (avoid taxes/penalties)
4. Choose investments in IRA
## Teachers & Public Employees: Special Considerations
keyPoints: Open IRA with low-cost provider (Vanguard, Fidelity, Schwab)
Never touch the money yourself (avoid taxes/penalties)

title: Pension + 403(b):
content:
Many teachers have BOTH:
- Defined benefit pension (guaranteed income)
- 403(b) (your own savings)
- This is powerful combination!

title: With a pension:
content:
- May need less in 403(b)
- But pension won't adjust for inflation
- 403(b) provides flexibility pension doesn't
- Still save at least 10-15% of income
keyPoints: But pension won't adjust for inflation
Still save at least 10-15% of income

title: Summers Off:
content:
- Some teachers have 403(b) contributions continue year-round
- Some only during school year (higher per-check deduction)
- Plan accordingly for cash flow
## 403(b) vs Additional IRA
keyPoints: Some teachers have 403(b) contributions continue year-round
Some only during school year (higher per-check deduction)

title: Yes, if possible:
content:
- Max 403(b) to get employer match
- Max Roth IRA ($7,000/year)
- Return to 403(b) with remaining savings

title: Why Roth IRA in addition:
content:
- Lower fees than many 403(b)s
- More investment choices
- Can withdraw contributions anytime
- No required minimum distributions
**Total retirement savings goal:** 15-20% of gross income
## Success Metrics

title: You're on track if:
content:
- ✓ Contributing at least to get full match
- ✓ Saving 10-15% of gross income for retirement
- ✓ Choosing low-cost index funds
- ✓ Increasing contributions with raises
- ✓ Not cashing out when changing jobs
keyPoints: ✓ Contributing at least to get full match
✓ Saving 10-15% of gross income for retirement
✓ Not cashing out when changing jobs

title: Warning signs:
content:
- ❌ Not participating at all
- ❌ Missing employer match
- ❌ Paying over 1% in fees
- ❌ Cashing out old accounts
- ❌ Only contributing minimum
## Real-Life Example

title: Sarah - 3rd Grade Teacher:
content:
- Age: 28
- Salary: $55,000
- Employer matches 4% (she contributes 4%)

title: Current contributions:
content:
- Sarah: $2,200/year (4% of $55,000)
- Employer: $2,200/year (4% match)
- Total: $4,400/year

title: At age 65 (37 years):
content:
- At 7% return: $687,000
- This is from just getting the match!
keyPoints: This is from just getting the match!

title: If Sarah increases to 10%:
content:
- Sarah: $5,500/year
- Employer: $2,200/year
- Total: $7,700/year
- **At age 65: $1,203,000**

title: The difference of 6% more = $516,000 more in retirement!
content: ## Action Steps

title: This Week:
content:
1. Check if your employer offers 403(b)
2. Find out if there's an employer match
3. Enroll to get at least the match
4. Choose low-cost index fund or target-date fund
keyPoints: Find out if there's an employer match
Enroll to get at least the match
Choose low-cost index fund or target-date fund

title: This Month:
content:
1. Review investment options and fees
2. Calculate 15% of your gross income
3. Increase contribution if below 15%
4. Set reminder to increase 1% next year
keyPoints: Set reminder to increase 1% next year

title: This Year:
content:
1. Review beneficiaries
2. Check on old 403(b) accounts
3. Consider consolidating to IRA
4. Evaluate Traditional vs Roth strategy
## Remember
Your 403(b) is one of the most powerful tools for building retirement security. As a teacher, healthcare worker, or non-profit employee, you've chosen a career of service—make sure you're taking care of your own future too.

title: Introduction
content:
Annuities are insurance products that can provide guaranteed income in retirement. They're complex, often expensive, but can be valuable in the right situation. This guide helps you understand if an annuity belongs in YOUR retirement plan.
## What is an Annuity?
**Simple definition:** A contract with an insurance company where you pay money now (or over time) in exchange for guaranteed income later.
**Think of it as:** Creating your own pension or Social Security-like payment for life.

title: The basic trade:
content:
- You give insurance company a lump sum (or series of payments)
- They promise to pay you income (monthly, quarterly, annually)
- Income can last for specific period or rest of your life
## Types of Annuities
### Fixed Annuities
keyPoints: You give insurance company a lump sum (or series of payments)
They promise to pay you income (monthly, quarterly, annually)
Income can last for specific period or rest of your life

title: How it works:
content:
- You get guaranteed fixed interest rate
- Income payments are predictable
- Insurance company takes investment risk
keyPoints: You get guaranteed fixed interest rate

title: Example:
content:
- Invest $200,000
- Get 4% annual return guaranteed
- Receive $8,000/year for life starting at 65

title: Pros:
content:
- Predictable, guaranteed returns
- No market risk
- Simple to understand

title: Cons:
content:
- Returns typically lower than market
- Doesn't keep up with inflation
- Money is locked up

title: Best for:
content:
- Conservative investors who prioritize safety
- Need predictable income
- Already maxed other retirement accounts
### Variable Annuities

title: How it works:
content:
- Your money invested in market (stocks/bonds)
- Returns vary based on market performance
- You take the investment risk
- Can have optional guarantees (for fees)
keyPoints: Returns vary based on market performance

title: Example:
content:
- Invest $200,000
- Money goes into mutual fund-like investments
- Account can grow or shrink
- Eventually convert to income stream
keyPoints: Money goes into mutual fund-like investments

title: Pros:
content:
- Potential for higher returns
- Tax-deferred growth
- Optional income guarantees

title: Cons:
content:
- High fees (2-3% annually)
- Complex products
- Surrender charges (can't get out for years)
- Risk of losing money
keyPoints: Surrender charges (can't get out for years)

title: Best for:
content:
- Wealthy investors already maxing other accounts
- Want market exposure with safety net
- Willing to pay for guarantees
### Indexed Annuities
keyPoints: Wealthy investors already maxing other accounts
Want market exposure with safety net

title: How it works:
content:
- Returns linked to stock market index (S&P 500)
- Has floor (usually 0%) and cap (often 5-8%)
- You get some upside, protected from downside
keyPoints: Returns linked to stock market index (S&P 500)
You get some upside, protected from downside

title: Example:
content:
- Invest $200,000
- Linked to S&P 500 with 7% cap
- If S&P gains 15%, you get 7%
- If S&P loses 20%, you get 0%

title: Pros:
content:
- Market participation with downside protection
- Can be less expensive than variable
- Tax-deferred growth
keyPoints: Can be less expensive than variable

title: Cons:
content:
- Capped upside (miss big market gains)
- Complex crediting formulas
- Surrender charges
- Often sold aggressively

title: Best for:
content:
- Want market exposure without losses
- Close to retirement
- Already maxed simpler options
- Fully understand the product
## Immediate vs Deferred Annuities

title: Immediate Annuity:
content:
- Pay lump sum today
- Income starts immediately (within 1 year)
- Can't change your mind
- Best at or in retirement

title: Deferred Annuity:
content:
- Pay now (lump sum or over time)
- Income starts later (years away)
- Money grows tax-deferred
- More common for pre-retirees
## Annuity Payout Options
keyPoints: Pay now (lump sum or over time)

title: Life with Period Certain:
content:
- Pays for your life
- Guaranteed minimum period (10, 20 years)
- If you die early, beneficiary gets remaining
- Lower payment than life only
keyPoints: If you die early, beneficiary gets remaining

title: Joint and Survivor:
content:
- Continues paying to spouse after you die
- Can be 100% or 50% to survivor
- Lower initial payment
- Best for married couples
keyPoints: Continues paying to spouse after you die

title: Period Certain Only:
content:
- Pays for specific period (10, 20 years)
- Then stops
- Not true lifetime income
- Predictable but not pension-like
## The Real Cost of Annuities

title: Surrender Charges:
content:
- Can't get your money out for 5-10 years
- Early withdrawal = penalties of 7-10%
- Decreases over time
- Locks up your money
keyPoints: Can't get your money out for 5-10 years

title: Mortality and Expense (M&E) Charges:
content:
- Annual fee (1-1.5%)
- Covers insurance company's risk
- Variable annuities have these

title: Investment Management Fees:
content:
- Fees on underlying investments
- Additional 0.5-1.5% annually
- Similar to mutual fund fees

title: Rider Fees:
content:
- Optional guarantees (death benefit, income)
- Each rider costs 0.25-1% annually
- Can add up quickly

title: Compare to:
content:
- Low-cost index fund: 0.05% ($100/year)
- Target-date fund: 0.15% ($300/year)
## When Annuities Make Sense

title: 1. You've maxed other retirement accounts
content:
- 401(k) and IRA both maxed out
- Have taxable brokerage money to invest
- Annuity offers tax-deferred growth
keyPoints: Have taxable brokerage money to invest

title: 2. You need guaranteed income
content:
- Anxious about market volatility
- No pension or Social Security
- Want to ensure basic expenses covered
keyPoints: Want to ensure basic expenses covered

title: 3. Longevity insurance
content:
- Family history of long life
- Worried about outliving savings
- Want pension-like security

title: 4. Tax-deferred growth for wealthy
content:
- High income tax bracket
- Want to defer taxes
- Not worried about high fees

title: 5. Legacy planning with guarantees
content:
- Want to ensure spouse has income
- Need death benefit guarantees
- Estate planning purposes
## When to Avoid Annuities
keyPoints: Want to ensure spouse has income

title: 1. Haven't maxed basic retirement accounts
content:
- 401(k) and IRA have better tax benefits
- Lower fees
- More flexibility
- **Fix this FIRST**
keyPoints: 401(k) and IRA have better tax benefits

title: 2. You need liquidity
content:
- Might need money in next 10 years
- No emergency fund
- Annuities lock up cash
keyPoints: Might need money in next 10 years

title: 3. You're young (under 50)
content:
- Too early to lock in rates
- Decades of compound growth ahead
- Market investment likely better
keyPoints: Too early to lock in rates

title: 4. You don't understand the product
content:
- If you can't explain it, don't buy it
- Too complex = probably too expensive
- Salespeople often oversell benefits
keyPoints: If you can't explain it, don't buy it

title: 5. You're being sold hard
content:
- High-pressure sales tactics
- "Limited time" offers
- Promises sound too good

title: 6. Fees are above 1%
content:
- Total annual fees over 1%
- Better options available
- Fee-only advisor can help compare
## Annuities vs DIY Retirement Income

title: DIY Approach:
content:
- Invest in low-cost index funds
- Follow 4% withdrawal rule
- Keep control of principal
- Leave money to heirs

title: DIY option:
content:
- 4% withdrawal: $20,000/year
- Can increase for inflation
- Principal can still grow
- Can leave $500k+ to heirs
- Full control
**The question:** Is $5,200 extra per year worth giving up control and inheritance?
## Annuity-Like Alternatives

title: 1. Social Security Delay
content:
- Delay to age 70 = 24% higher benefit
- Guaranteed, inflation-adjusted
- Free (no fees)
- Often better than annuity

title: 2. Bond Ladder
content:
- Buy bonds maturing in sequence
- Creates income stream
- Lower fees
- More flexibility

title: 3. Dividend Stocks
content:
- Blue-chip dividend aristocrats
- Growing income over time
- Principal can grow
- Keeps up with inflation

title: 4. TIPS (Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities)
content:
- Inflation-adjusted returns
- Government guaranteed
- Lower fees than annuities
## Questions to Ask Before Buying

title: Product questions:
content:
1. What are the total annual fees?
2. What's the surrender charge period and amount?
3. What exactly is guaranteed?
4. How is return calculated?
5. What happens if I die early?
keyPoints: What are the total annual fees?
What's the surrender charge period and amount?
What happens if I die early?

title: Situation questions:
content:
1. Have I maxed 401(k) and IRA?
2. Do I have 6+ months emergency fund?
3. Am I at least 55+ years old?
4. Do I need this money in next 10 years?
5. Will I regret locking this up?
keyPoints: Do I have 6+ months emergency fund?
Am I at least 55+ years old?
Do I need this money in next 10 years?
Will I regret locking this up?

title: Seller questions:
content:
1. How are you compensated? (commission)
2. What's your Series 65 or CFP status?
3. Can I have a week to review with my advisor?
4. What are comparable products?
5. Why this specific annuity?
## Working with Annuity Salespeople
keyPoints: What's your Series 65 or CFP status?
Can I have a week to review with my advisor?

title: Red flags:
content:
- Pushes you to decide quickly
- Won't disclose commissions
- Says it's "free" (it's not)
- Compares to savings accounts (unfair comparison)
- Promises market returns with no risk
- Focuses on tax-deferral only
keyPoints: Promises market returns with no risk

title: Green flags:
content:
- Encourages you to review with fee-only advisor
- Discloses all fees upfront
- Explains surrender charges clearly
- Provides comparison illustrations
- Discusses alternatives
- Doesn't pressure
keyPoints: Encourages you to review with fee-only advisor

title: Best practice:
content:
- Get recommendation from FEE-ONLY fiduciary advisor
- One who doesn't sell annuities
- Pay for advice (often worth it)
- Avoid commission-based salespeople
## The Verdict: Are Annuities Worth It?
keyPoints: Get recommendation from FEE-ONLY fiduciary advisor

title: For most people: NO
content:
- Fees are too high
- Better to max 401(k), IRA first
- DIY retirement income works fine
- Gives up too much control

title: For some people: YES
content:
- Already maxed all retirement accounts
- Need guaranteed income for peace of mind
- High tax bracket (defer taxes)
- No heirs to leave money to
- Family history of longevity
**The key:** Understand exactly what you're buying and why it's better than alternatives.
## Action Steps
keyPoints: Need guaranteed income for peace of mind
No heirs to leave money to

title: Before considering annuity:
content:
1. Max out 401(k) ($23,000/year)
2. Max out IRA ($7,000/year)
3. Build 12-month emergency fund
4. Pay off high-interest debt
5. Have taxable brokerage money

title: If still interested:
content:
1. Get quotes from 3+ companies
2. Compare total fees
3. Hire fee-only CFP for review ($300-500 well spent)
4. Read prospectus cover-to-cover
5. Sleep on decision for at least 30 days
## Remember
Annuities aren't inherently good or bad—they're tools. Like any tool, they work great in the right situation and terrible in the wrong one.
keyPoints: Hire fee-only CFP for review ($300-500 well spent)
Sleep on decision for at least 30 days

title: Most people are better off with:
content:
- Low-cost index funds

title: Introduction
content:
The most powerful retirement strategies don't rely on a single account or product—they combine multiple tools working together. This guide shows you how to build a comprehensive retirement plan using everything available to you.
## The Retirement Planning Pyramid
Think of retirement planning as building a financial pyramid, from foundation to peak:

title: Level 1 (Foundation): Basic Retirement Accounts
content:
- 401(k) or 403(b) with employer match
- Roth IRA
- HSA (if available)
- Emergency fund (6-12 months)

title: Level 2: Max Basic Accounts
content:
- Max 401(k)/403(b) ($23,000/year)
- Max Roth IRA ($7,000/year)
- Max HSA ($4,150-$8,300)
- Pay off high-interest debt

title: Level 3: Taxable Investments
content:
- Brokerage account with index funds
- Real estate investments
- Business ownership

title: Level 4: Advanced Strategies
content:
- Mega backdoor Roth
- Tax-loss harvesting
- Asset location optimization

title: Level 5: Specialized Products
content:
- Annuities (for guarantees)
- IUL (for tax-free income + insurance)
- Alternative investments

title: You must build from bottom up—don't skip levels!
content: ## Your 20s-30s: Foundation Building

title: Priority order:
content:
1. **Emergency fund** (3-6 months of expenses)
2. **401(k) to get employer match** (free money!)
3. **Pay off high-interest debt** (credit cards, etc.)
4. **Max Roth IRA** ($7,000/year)
5. **Increase 401(k)** (work toward $23,000)
6. **Save 15-20% of gross income** total

title: Investment strategy:
content:
- 90% stocks, 10% bonds (aggressive growth)
- Target-date funds or simple 3-fund portfolio
- Don't touch it for decades

title: Consider if wealthy:
content:
- HSA as retirement account (max it, invest it)
- Taxable brokerage for extra savings
- Real estate if you understand it
keyPoints: HSA as retirement account (max it, invest it)
Real estate if you understand it

title: Skip for now:
content:
- Annuities (too young)
- IUL (maybe if very high income + need insurance)
- Complicated strategies
keyPoints: IUL (maybe if very high income + need insurance)

title: Real example - Age 28, $65k salary:
content:
- Emergency fund: $15,000
- 401(k): 15% ($9,750 + $3,250 match = $13,000/year)
- Roth IRA: $7,000/year
- **Total saved: $20,000/year (31% of income)**
- At 65: $3.2 million (at 7% return)
## Your 40s: Acceleration Phase

title: Priority order:
content:
1. **Increase 401(k) contributions** (aim for max)
2. **Continue maxing Roth IRA**
3. **Start taxable brokerage** if retirement accounts maxed
4. **Consider tax diversification**
5. **Review insurance needs**
keyPoints: **Start taxable brokerage** if retirement accounts maxed

title: Investment strategy:
content:
- 80% stocks, 20% bonds
- Start thinking about tax diversification
- Consider Roth conversions if in lower tax year
keyPoints: Consider Roth conversions if in lower tax year

title: New considerations:
content:
- **529 plans** for kids' education (if applicable)
- **Real estate** if financially stable
- **Side business** for additional retirement savings (SEP IRA, Solo 401k)
- **Tax optimization** becomes more important
keyPoints: **Side business** for additional retirement savings (SEP IRA, Solo 401k)

title: Maybe consider:
content:
- IUL if high income, maxing accounts, need insurance
- Annuities NOT recommended yet (still too young)
keyPoints: IUL if high income, maxing accounts, need insurance
Annuities NOT recommended yet (still too young)

title: Real example - Age 45, $120k salary:
content:
- Max 401(k): $23,000
- Max Roth IRA: $7,000
- Taxable brokerage: $10,000

title: - HSA: $4,150
content:
- **Total: $44,150/year (37% of income)**
- Plus existing $300k in retirement accounts
- At 65: $2.5 million
## Your 50s: Peak Earning & Catch-Up

title: 7. Annuity Payments (if you bought)
content:
- Guaranteed income for life
- Partially taxable
- Provides floor of income

title: Priority order:
content:
1. **Max everything** (use catch-up contributions)
2. **Aggressive savings** (20-25% of income)
3. **Tax optimization** (Roth conversions, harvesting)
4. **Retirement income planning**

title: Catch-up contributions:
content:
- 401(k): Extra $7,500 (total $30,500)
- IRA: Extra $1,000 (total $8,000)
- HSA: No catch-up (but max it anyway)
keyPoints: HSA: No catch-up (but max it anyway)

title: Investment strategy:
content:
- 70% stocks, 30% bonds
- Start shift toward conservation
- But still need growth (could live 30+ more years)
keyPoints: But still need growth (could live 30+ more years)

title: New focus:
content:
- **Define retirement number** (how much you need)
- **Project Social Security** (visit ssa.gov)
- **Healthcare planning** (bridge to Medicare at 65)
- **Long-term care** consideration

title: Consider now:
content:
- **Annuities** for portion of portfolio (guarantees)
- **IUL** if high income and haven't started (still time)
- **Roth conversions** (before RMDs start)
keyPoints: **IUL** if high income and haven't started (still time)

title: Real example - Age 55, $150k salary:
content:
- Max 401(k) with catch-up: $30,500
- Max Roth IRA with catch-up: $8,000
- Taxable brokerage: $15,000

title: - HSA: $4,150
content:
- **Total: $57,650/year (38% of income)**
- Existing $800k in accounts
- At 65: $2.2 million
## Your 60s: Transition & Optimization

title: Priority order:
content:
1. **Continue maxing accounts** (every dollar counts)
2. **Finalize retirement date**
3. **Social Security strategy**
4. **Medicare planning**
5. **Income withdrawal strategy**

title: Investment strategy:
content:
- 60% stocks, 40% bonds
- Preserve capital but still need growth
- Build "bucket strategy" (see below)
keyPoints: Preserve capital but still need growth

title: Critical decisions:
content:
- **When to retire** (even 1-2 extra years huge)
- **When to claim Social Security** (delay to 70 if possible)
- **Medicare enrollment** (don't miss deadlines!)
- **Roth conversion ladder** (before RMDs at 73)
keyPoints: **When to claim Social Security** (delay to 70 if possible)

title: Consider strongly:
content:
- **Immediate annuity** for portion (guaranteed income)
- **Review IUL policies** if you have them
- **Simplify investments** (fewer accounts easier)
- **Long-term care insurance** (or self-insure with assets)
keyPoints: **Review IUL policies** if you have them

title: Real example - Age 65 retiring:
content:
- Retirement accounts: $1.5 million
- Taxable brokerage: $300,000
- Home equity: $400,000
- **Total: $2.2 million**
- 4% rule: $88,000/year
- Social Security: $30,000/year
- **Total income: $118,000/year**
## The Complete Retirement Income Plan

title: 1. Social Security
content:
- Start: Age 62-70 (optimize based on situation)
- Average: $1,500-3,000/month
- Inflation-adjusted for life
- Tax treatment: 0-85% taxable

title: 2. Pension (if you have one)
content:
- Guaranteed income for life
- Usually inflation-adjusted
- Rare but valuable

title: 3. 401(k)/403(b)/IRA Withdrawals
content:
- Required minimum distributions (RMDs) at 73
- Taxable as ordinary income
- Strategic withdrawals to minimize taxes

title: 4. Roth IRA Withdrawals
content:
- Tax-free income
- No RMDs (keep growing forever)
- Withdraw last for maximum growth

title: 5. Taxable Brokerage
content:
- Flexible withdrawals anytime
- Capital gains treatment (lower tax)
- Use for early retirement (before 59½)

title: 6. HSA Withdrawals
content:
- Tax-free for medical expenses
- After 65: can use for anything (taxed like IRA)
- Medical expenses in retirement: $300k+ for couple
keyPoints: After 65: can use for anything (taxed like IRA)
Medical expenses in retirement: $300k+ for couple

title: 8. IUL Policy Loans (if you have policy)
content:
- Tax-free loans against cash value
- Supplement other income
- Strategically manage to avoid lapse

title: 9. Part-time Work
content:
- Consulting, hobbies that pay
- Reduces withdrawal stress
- Social and mental benefits

title: 10. Real Estate Income
content:
- Rental properties
- REITs in portfolio
- Reverse mortgage (last resort)
## Withdrawal Strategies
### The 4% Rule

title: Classic approach:
content:
- Withdraw 4% of portfolio in year 1
- Adjust for inflation each year
- Portfolio should last 30+ years
- Based on historical data

title: Example:
content:
- $1 million portfolio
- Year 1: Withdraw $40,000
- Year 2: Withdraw $40,800 (adjusted for 2% inflation)
- Continue for life
**Pros:** Simple, historically safe
**Cons:** Rigid, doesn't adapt to market
### The Bucket Strategy

title: Bucket 1 (Cash/Bonds): 2-3 years expenses
content:
- High-yield savings
- Short-term bonds
- Live off this during bear markets
- Refill from Bucket 2 in good years
keyPoints: Live off this during bear markets
Refill from Bucket 2 in good years

title: Bucket 2 (Moderate): 5-10 years expenses
content:
- Balanced portfolio (60/40)
- Bond ladder
- Dividend stocks
- Refills Bucket 1

title: Bucket 3 (Growth): 10+ years out
content:
- Aggressive stocks
- Growth investments
- Never touched during bear markets
- Refills Bucket 2 over time
**Advantage:** Never forced to sell stocks in down market
### The Tax-Efficient Strategy

title: Age 60-62 (pre-Social Security):
content:
1. Taxable brokerage (capital gains rates)
2. Traditional IRA (fill low tax brackets)
3. Roth IRA (never, save for later)

title: Age 62-70 (delaying Social Security):
content:
1. Taxable brokerage
2. Traditional IRA conversions to Roth (while in lower bracket)
3. Live off converted Roth in future
keyPoints: Traditional IRA conversions to Roth (while in lower bracket)
Live off converted Roth in future

title: Age 70+ (Social Security started):
content:
1. Social Security (required)
2. RMDs from traditional IRA (required at 73)
3. Taxable brokerage to fill tax bracket
4. Roth IRA if needed (tax-free bonus)
**Goal:** Pay least total taxes over lifetime

## Common Complete Strategy Mistakes

title: Mistake 1: All Eggs in 401(k)
content:
- 100% in traditional = 100% taxable in retirement
- Solution: Mix of traditional, Roth, taxable, maybe IUL
keyPoints: Solution: Mix of traditional, Roth, taxable, maybe IUL

title: Mistake 2: Ignoring Taxes
content:
- Not planning for RMDs
- All withdrawals from high-tax accounts
- Solution: Tax diversification and Roth conversions
keyPoints: Solution: Tax diversification and Roth conversions

title: Mistake 3: Too Conservative Too Soon
content:
- Moving to 100% bonds at 60
- Could live 30+ more years
- Solution: Keep 50-60% stocks even in retirement
keyPoints: Solution: Keep 50-60% stocks even in retirement

title: Mistake 4: Claiming Social Security Too Early
content:
- Taking at 62 locks in 25-30% less
- Every year delayed = 8% increase
- Solution: Delay to 70 if possible

title: Mistake 5: No Guaranteed Income Floor
content:
- All assets in market
- Anxiety during downturns
- Solution: Social Security + maybe small annuity
keyPoints: Solution: Social Security + maybe small annuity

title: Mistake 6: Forgetting Healthcare
content:
- Medicare doesn't cover everything
- Long-term care can cost $100k+/year
- Solution: HSA, long-term care insurance, or self-insure
keyPoints: Solution: HSA, long-term care insurance, or self-insure

title: Mistake 7: One-Size-Fits-All
content:
- Following generic advice
- Not customizing to your situation
- Solution: Get professional advice for complex situations
## When to Get Professional Help
keyPoints: Solution: Get professional advice for complex situations

- Single employer 401(k)
- Straightforward income
- One retirement account type
- Under $500k in assets
- No complicated tax situation

- Multiple income sources

- Complex tax situation

content:
- Hourly: $200-400/hour
- Project fee: $2,000-5,000
- Ongoing: 0.5-1% of assets annually

title: Worth it if:

- They save more in taxes than they cost

- Avoid expensive mistakes

title: Introduction
content:
Index Universal Life (IUL) insurance with living benefits is a powerful financial tool combining life insurance, tax-advantaged savings, and health coverage. What was once reserved for high earners is now accessible to people of all income levels—allowing families to plan early for major life events, college expenses, and secure lifetime income.
## What is Index Universal Life (IUL)?

title: IUL combines three components:
content:
1. **Permanent life insurance** - Death benefit for beneficiaries
2. **Cash value account** - Grows tax-deferred linked to market index
3. **Flexibility** - Adjust premiums and death benefit
keyPoints: **Permanent life insurance** - Death benefit for beneficiaries
**Cash value account** - Grows tax-deferred linked to market index

title: Different from term life:
content:
- Term = temporary coverage, no cash value
- IUL = permanent coverage + investment component
keyPoints: Term = temporary coverage, no cash value

title: Different from whole life:
content:
- Whole life = fixed returns, guaranteed values
- IUL = returns linked to stock index (S&P 500), not guaranteed
## How People Use IUL Today
keyPoints: Whole life = fixed returns, guaranteed values
IUL = returns linked to stock index (S&P 500), not guaranteed

title: College Planning:
content:
- Build cash value over 10-18 years
- Access funds tax-free via loans for tuition
- More flexible than 529 plans (can use for anything)
- If child doesn't go to college, money still available
keyPoints: Access funds tax-free via loans for tuition
More flexible than 529 plans (can use for anything)
If child doesn't go to college, money still available

title: Major Life Purchases:
content:
- Down payment for home
- Wedding expenses
- Starting a business
- Emergency backup fund
- Vehicle purchases

title: Lifetime Income Planning:
content:
- Lifetime benefit income rider acts like a pension
- Guaranteed income that NEVER runs out
- Supplements Social Security and retirement accounts
- Provides security in uncertain times
**The key:** People of all income levels are securing these early to maximize growth time and lock in lower insurance costs while young and healthy.
## How Indexed Growth Works
keyPoints: Lifetime benefit income rider acts like a pension
Guaranteed income that NEVER runs out
Supplements Social Security and retirement accounts

title: The mechanism:
content:
- Your cash value is linked to stock market index (typically S&P 500)
- Has FLOOR (usually 0% or 1%) - you can't lose money
- Has CAP (typically 10-12%) - limits upside
- Resets annually
keyPoints: Your cash value is linked to stock market index (typically S&P 500)
Has FLOOR (usually 0% or 1%) - you can't lose money

title: Example year:
content:
- S&P 500 gains 25%
- Your cap is 10%
- You get 10% (capped)

title: The trade-off:
content:
- Give up some upside (capping gains)
- Eliminate downside (floor protection)
- Over time, competitive with market with less volatility
keyPoints: Over time, competitive with market with less volatility

title: Historical performance:
content:
- Long-term average: 6-8% annually
- Better than whole life (3-5%)
- Protected from market crashes
- Predictable growth over time
## The Lifetime Benefit Income Rider

title: How it works:
content:
- Optional rider added to IUL policy
- Guarantees lifetime income payments
- Income NEVER runs out (like a pension)
- Payments continue for life regardless of account balance
keyPoints: Optional rider added to IUL policy
Income NEVER runs out (like a pension)
Payments continue for life regardless of account balance

title: Example scenario:
content:
- Start IUL at age 30 with income rider
- Fund for 30 years
- At age 60, activate income
- Receive $2,500/month for LIFE
- Even if cash value depletes, payments continue
- Death benefit still pays to beneficiaries
keyPoints: Start IUL at age 30 with income rider
Even if cash value depletes, payments continue

title: Why this matters:
content:
- Most pensions are gone
- Social Security alone isn't enough
- Creates personal pension you control
- Security for entire retirement
- Peace of mind knowing income won't stop
keyPoints: Peace of mind knowing income won't stop

title: Unlike other retirement accounts:
content:
- 401(k) can run out
- IRA can run out
- Lifetime income rider NEVER runs out
- Guaranteed by insurance company
## What Are Living Benefits?
**Traditional life insurance:** Only pays when you die
**Living benefits:** Access death benefit WHILE YOU'RE ALIVE for qualifying events
### Types of Living Benefit Riders
keyPoints: Lifetime income rider NEVER runs out

title: 1. Terminal Illness Rider
content:
- Diagnosed with <12-24 months to live
- Access 25-100% of death benefit
- Use for medical care, final expenses
- Most policies include this FREE
keyPoints: Use for medical care, final expenses

title: 2. Critical Illness Rider
content:
- Lump sum payment upon diagnosis:
  - Heart attack
  - Stroke
  - Cancer
  - Major organ transplant
  - Kidney failure
- Typical payout: 25-100% of death benefit
- Can use for any purpose
- Helps cover medical costs, lost income

title: 3. Chronic Illness Rider
content:
- Can't perform 2+ "Activities of Daily Living" (ADLs)
- Access death benefit to pay for care
- Alternative to long-term care insurance
- Monthly payments while chronically ill
keyPoints: Access death benefit to pay for care

title: 4. Long-Term Care Rider
content:
- Pays for nursing home, assisted living, in-home care
- Accelerates death benefit
- Can be substantial (2-3x death benefit in some policies)
**The power:** You're not just insuring for death—you're creating safety net for health events that could devastate finances while you're still alive.

Most people save what's left after spending. Wealthy people save first, then spend what remains.
### The Strategy
Treat savings like a non-negotiable bill. Set up automatic transfers on payday before you can spend the money. Start with just 5-10% if needed.
### Why It Works
- Removes willpower from the equation- Builds wealth automatically- Adjusts spending to available funds- Creates positive habit momentum
### Implementation Steps
- Calculate savings percentage goal- Set up automatic transfer on payday- Direct to separate savings account- Increase percentage with raises

title: Introduction
content:
## Give Every Dollar a Purpose
Zero-based budgeting means your income minus all planned expenses equals zero. Every dollar is assigned a specific role before the month begins.
### How It Works
- Write down your monthly income- List all expenses and savings goals- Assign amounts until you reach zero- Adjust categories as needed
### Benefits
- Complete awareness of money flow- Eliminates mystery spending- Prioritizes financial goals- Reveals spending patterns
### Common Pitfall
Don't forget to budget for irregular expenses like annual insurance premiums or holiday gifts. Set aside money monthly to avoid surprises.
keyPoints: Write down your monthly income- List all expenses and savings goals- Assign amounts until you reach zero- Adjust categories as needed
Complete awareness of money flow- Eliminates mystery spending- Prioritizes financial goals- Reveals spending patterns

title: Introduction
content:
## Cash in Envelopes Works
The envelope method divides cash into category-specific envelopes. When an envelope is empty, spending stops in that category.
### How It Works
- Create envelopes for variable expenses (groceries, dining out, entertainment)- Fill with budgeted cash at month start- Spend only from designated envelopes- Stop when envelope is empty
### Digital Version
Use CoinsBloom's virtual envelopes or separate savings accounts for each category. Transfer budgeted amounts at the start of each month.
keyPoints: Create envelopes for variable expenses (groceries, dining out, entertainment)- Fill with budgeted cash at month start- Spend only from designated envelopes- Stop when envelope is empty

title: Introduction
content:
## Taming the Variable Income
Freelancers, contractors, and commission-based workers need special budgeting strategies to handle income fluctuations.
### Key Strategies
- **Budget on lowest month:** Use your minimum expected income- **Build a larger buffer:** Aim for 6+ months expenses- **Priority-based spending:** List expenses by importance- **Income smoothing:** Pay yourself a consistent salary
### The Holding Account
Deposit all income into a holding account, then transfer your fixed "salary" to checking monthly. Extra income stays in the buffer for lean months.

title: Introduction
content:
## The Monthly Money Meeting
Successful budgeters schedule regular reviews to stay on track and adjust as needed.
### Review Checklist
- Compare budgeted vs. actual spending- Identify overspent categories- Celebrate wins and progress- Adjust next month based on learnings- Check progress toward financial goals
### Make It a Ritual
Choose the same day each month (last day works well). Set aside 30 minutes. Make it pleasant with coffee or tea. Review alone or with household members involved in finances.
keyPoints: Compare budgeted vs. actual spending- Identify overspent categories- Celebrate wins and progress- Adjust next month based on learnings- Check progress toward financial goals

title: Introduction
content:

Automation removes the decision fatigue and temptation from saving. Your wealth grows on autopilot.
### What to Automate
- **Emergency fund:** Transfer to savings on payday- **Retirement:** 401(k) or IRA contributions- **Short-term goals:** Vacation, car replacement- **Bills:** Never miss a payment
### Implementation
- Review payday schedule- Set up automatic transfers day after payday- Use employer payroll split if available- Start small and increase over time
### The Psychology Win
You never see the money, so you don't miss it. Spending adjusts automatically to what's available.

content:
## Goals Drive Behavior
Vague goals like "save more" rarely work. Specific, emotional goals with deadlines create powerful motivation.
### SMART Goals Framework
- **Specific:** "Save $5,000" not "save money"- **Measurable:** Track progress weekly- **Achievable:** Stretch but realistic- **Relevant:** Truly matters to you- **Time-bound:** Clear deadline
### Emotional Connection
Add photos and descriptions to goals in CoinsBloom. Visualize the outcome. Why does this goal matter? How will achieving it feel?
### Milestone Celebrations
Break large goals into milestones and celebrate progress. Reward yourself (reasonably) when you hit 25%, 50%, 75% completion.
keyPoints: **Specific:** "Save $5,000" not "save money"- **Measurable:** Track progress weekly- **Achievable:** Stretch but realistic- **Relevant:** Truly matters to you- **Time-bound:** Clear deadline

title: Introduction
content:
## Predictable "Surprises"
Sinking funds are savings accounts for expenses you know are coming but don't happen monthly.
### Common Sinking Funds
- Annual insurance premiums- Car maintenance and registration- Holiday and birthday gifts- Home repairs and maintenance- Medical deductibles- Vacation travel
### How to Calculate
Divide annual expense by 12 months. Save that amount monthly. When the bill comes, you have money ready.
### Example
Car insurance: $1,200/year ÷ 12 = $100/month into car insurance sinking fund. No panic when the bill arrives.
keyPoints: Annual insurance premiums- Car maintenance and registration- Holiday and birthday gifts- Home repairs and maintenance- Medical deductibles- Vacation travel

title: Introduction
content:
## Don't Let Windfalls Vanish
Most people spend unexpected money quickly and have nothing to show for it. Strategic use of windfalls accelerates financial goals.
### The 50/30/20 Windfall Rule
- **50% to financial goals:** Debt, emergency fund, retirement- **30% to desires:** Something you've been wanting- **20% to give/experience:** Generosity or memories
### Priority Waterfall
- High-interest debt (over 7% APR)- Complete 3-6 month emergency fund- Tax-advantaged retirement accounts- Other financial goals- Treat yourself responsibly
keyPoints: **50% to financial goals:** Debt, emergency fund, retirement- **30% to desires:** Something you've been wanting- **20% to give/experience:** Generosity or memories
High-interest debt (over 7% APR)- Complete 3-6 month emergency fund- Tax-advantaged retirement accounts- Other financial goals- Treat yourself responsibly

title: Introduction
content:
## Never Miss a Due Date
Automation guarantees on-time payments, protects your credit score, and eliminates mental burden.
### What to Automate
- **Always automate:** Fixed bills (rent, insurance, loan payments)- **Consider automating:** Minimum credit card payments as backup- **Don't automate:** Variable bills you want to review (utilities, phone)
### Setup Steps
- Verify checking account balance timing- Set up auto-pay through biller's website- Choose payment date 2-3 days before due date- Keep email confirmations- Review statements monthly for errors
### Safety Tips
Maintain bill buffer in checking account. Still review statements. Cancel automation if changing banks.
keyPoints: Verify checking account balance timing- Set up auto-pay through biller's website- Choose payment date 2-3 days before due date- Keep email confirmations- Review statements monthly for errors

title: Introduction
content:
## The Hidden Money Leak
Average American has 4 forgotten subscriptions costing $200/month. Many sign up during free trials and forget to cancel.
### Finding Hidden Subscriptions
- Review 3 months of credit card and bank statements- Look for recurring charges- Check email for subscription confirmations- Use CoinsBloom's subscription detector
### Common Culprits
- Streaming services not actively used- App subscriptions after free trial- Magazine or box subscriptions- Gym memberships unused for months- Premium features never utilized
### Action Plan
Cancel anything unused in past 2 months. Downgrade premium features to free tiers. Set calendar reminders before free trials end.
keyPoints: Review 3 months of credit card and bank statements- Look for recurring charges- Check email for subscription confirmations- Use CoinsBloom's subscription detector
Streaming services not actively used- App subscriptions after free trial- Magazine or box subscriptions- Gym memberships unused for months- Premium features never utilized

title: Introduction
content:
## The Annual Discount
Many services offer 15-20% discounts for annual payment, but it's not always the best choice.
### When to Pay Annually
- Services you're committed to using all year- Discounts of 15%+ offered- You have emergency fund established- Cash flow comfortably supports it
### When to Pay Monthly
- New services you're testing- Tight cash flow situations- Service quality uncertain- Better to invest the lump sum elsewhere
### Real Example
Streaming service: $12/month ($144/year) vs $99/year annual. Annual saves $45 (31% discount). If you'll use it all year, annual is smart.
### Pro Tip
Set up sinking fund for annual subscriptions. Save monthly so you have funds ready when renewal hits.
keyPoints: Services you're committed to using all year- Discounts of 15%+ offered- You have emergency fund established- Cash flow comfortably supports it
New services you're testing- Tight cash flow situations- Service quality uncertain- Better to invest the lump sum elsewhere

title: Introduction
content:
## Control Your Cash Flow
Most billers will change due dates upon request. Strategic scheduling prevents overdrafts and stress.
### Ideal Setup
- Align major bills with paydays- Space out large payments- Pay fixed bills first, variables mid-month- Keep 3-5 days buffer after payday
### Implementation
- Map out paydays on calendar- Call billers to request due date changes- Move half of bills to each payday if paid twice monthly- Document new due dates
### Cash Flow Example
Paid on 1st and 15th? Schedule rent, car payment, and insurance near the 3rd. Schedule utilities, phone, and credit cards near the 17th.
keyPoints: Align major bills with paydays- Space out large payments- Pay fixed bills first, variables mid-month- Keep 3-5 days buffer after payday
Map out paydays on calendar- Call billers to request due date changes- Move half of bills to each payday if paid twice monthly- Document new due dates

title: Introduction
content:
## You Can't Do Everything at Once
Most people have more goals than available money. Strategic prioritization ensures steady progress without overwhelm.
### Priority Framework
- **Foundation:** $1,000 emergency fund + minimum debt payments- **High-interest debt:** Pay off anything over 7% APR- **Emergency fund:** Complete 3-6 months expenses- **Retirement match:** Get full employer 401(k) match- **Other goals:** Prioritize by importance and timeline
### Parallel Progress Strategy
Split savings between 2-3 goals maximum. Example: 50% emergency fund, 30% down payment, 20% vacation. Too many splits slow all progress.

title: Introduction
content:
## Measure What Matters
Regular progress tracking maintains motivation and allows for timely adjustments.
### Tracking Methods
- **CoinsBloom Goals:** Automatic balance tracking and visual progress- **Spreadsheet:** Manual entries with charts- **Journal:** Weekly written reflections- **Photos:** Visual documentation of milestones
### Review Frequency
Weekly: Quick check on short-term goals. Monthly: Detailed review of all goals. Quarterly: Assess priorities and adjust strategies. Annually: Major planning and goal-setting session.
### Celebration Points
Acknowledge every 25% milestone. Share wins with supportive friends/family. Reward yourself appropriately for major achievements.

title: Introduction
content:
## Your Financial North Star
Long-term vision provides context for daily decisions and prevents short-term thinking from derailing your future.
### Vision Questions
- Where do you want to live?- What does ideal workweek look like?- What experiences matter most?- Who do you want to help?- What legacy will you leave?
### Financial Freedom Numbers
Calculate your FI number: Annual expenses × 25 = amount needed invested to retire. Example: $50,000/year × 25 = $1.25M invested enables retirement.
### Reverse Engineering
Start with vision, identify required resources, break into 5-year milestones, then annual goals, then monthly actions.
keyPoints: Where do you want to live?- What does ideal workweek look like?- What experiences matter most?- Who do you want to help?- What legacy will you leave?

title: Introduction
content:
## The Risk-Return Tradeoff
Higher potential returns come with higher risk. Understanding this relationship helps you invest appropriately for your goals and timeline.
### Risk Spectrum
- **Lowest risk:** Savings accounts, CDs (1-2% return)- **Low risk:** Government bonds (2-4% return)- **Moderate risk:** Corporate bonds (4-6% return)- **Higher risk:** Stock index funds (8-10% average)- **Highest risk:** Individual stocks (highly variable)
### Your Risk Tolerance
Consider: time horizon (longer allows more risk), financial stability, emotional comfort with volatility, and goals.
### Age-Based Strategy
Young: Can take more risk with decades to recover from downturns. Near retirement: Need stability to preserve wealth. Common rule: Hold (100 - age)% in stocks.

title: Introduction
content:
## Never Leave Free Money Behind
Employer 401(k) match is guaranteed 100% return on your investment. It's the best deal in investing.
### How It Works
Common match: Employer contributes 50-100% of your contributions up to 3-6% of salary. If you earn $50,000 and contribute 6% ($3,000), employer adds $1,500-$3,000 free money.
### Tax Advantages
- **Traditional 401(k):** Contributions reduce taxable income now, pay taxes in retirement- **Roth 401(k):** Pay taxes now, withdrawals tax-free in retirement
### Investment Strategy
At minimum, contribute enough to get full match. Choose target-date fund matching retirement year for simple diversification. Increase contributions 1% annually.
keyPoints: **Traditional 401(k):** Contributions reduce taxable income now, pay taxes in retirement- **Roth 401(k):** Pay taxes now, withdrawals tax-free in retirement

title: Introduction
content:
## The Evidence Is Clear
Studies show 90% of active fund managers underperform index funds over 15 years, after fees. Index investing is the smart choice for most investors.
### Index Fund Advantages
- **Lower fees:** 0.03-0.20% vs 1-2% for active funds- **Diversification:** Own hundreds or thousands of stocks- **Consistent returns:** Match market performance guaranteed- **Less time:** No research or monitoring needed- **Tax efficient:** Less trading means fewer capital gains
### Recommended Strategy
Invest in total stock market index fund (VTI, VTSAX) for US stocks. Add international index (VXUS) for global exposure. Add bond index (BND) as you near retirement.

title: Introduction
content:
## Reduce Risk Without Sacrificing Returns
Diversification spreads investments across different assets to reduce risk from any single investment failing.
### Types of Diversification
- **Asset classes:** Stocks, bonds, real estate- **Geographic:** US and international markets- **Company size:** Large, mid, and small cap- **Sectors:** Technology, healthcare, finance, etc.
### Simple Diversified Portfolio
Three-fund portfolio: Total US stock market (60%), total international stocks (30%), total bond market (10%). Rebalance annually to maintain ratios.
### Over-Diversification
Owning too many similar funds doesn't add value. Focus on broad index funds covering different asset classes.
keyPoints: **Asset classes:** Stocks, bonds, real estate- **Geographic:** US and international markets- **Company size:** Large, mid, and small cap- **Sectors:** Technology, healthcare, finance, etc.

title: Introduction
content:
## Your Personal Retirement Account
IRAs offer tax advantages for retirement savings. Anyone with earned income can contribute, regardless of employer plan access.
### Traditional IRA
- Contributions may be tax-deductible- Growth is tax-deferred- Pay taxes on withdrawals in retirement- Required minimum distributions at age 73
### Roth IRA
- Contributions with after-tax dollars- Growth is tax-free forever- Withdrawals tax-free in retirement- No required distributions- Can withdraw contributions anytime
### Contribution Limits (2025)
$7,000 per year ($8,000 if 50+). Roth has income limits. Open at brokers like Vanguard, Fidelity, or Schwab.
keyPoints: Contributions may be tax-deductible- Growth is tax-deferred- Pay taxes on withdrawals in retirement- Required minimum distributions at age 73
Contributions with after-tax dollars- Growth is tax-free forever- Withdrawals tax-free in retirement- No required distributions- Can withdraw contributions anytime

title: Introduction
content:
## Building Wealth Through Property
Real estate can generate passive income and appreciate over time, but requires more capital and management than stocks.
### Investment Strategies
- **Rental property:** Buy and rent out, generate monthly income- **House hacking:** Live in one unit, rent others to cover mortgage- **REITs:** Real estate stocks, no property management- **Crowdfunding:** Invest in commercial real estate with less capital
### Rental Property Math
1% Rule: Monthly rent should equal 1% of purchase price. $200,000 home should rent for $2,000/month to be good deal.
### Pros and Cons
Pros: Passive income, tax benefits, leverage with mortgage, tangible asset. Cons: Large capital needed, property management, illiquid, maintenance costs.

title: Benefits
content:
- Saves the most money in interest
- Mathematically optimal
- Faster debt payoff overall
- Better for high-interest debt
keyPoints: Saves the most money in interest

title: Introduction
content:
## Your First Step to Wealth
Starting to invest can feel intimidating. Here's exactly what to do with your first $1,000.
### Before Investing Your First $1,000
- Pay off high-interest debt (over 7% APR)- Have $1,000 starter emergency fund- Get employer 401(k) match if available
### If Requirements Met, Here's Your Plan
- **Open Roth IRA:** At Vanguard, Fidelity, or Schwab- **Invest in target-date fund:** Pick year you'll turn 65- **Set up automatic contributions:** Even $50/month adds up- **Don't check it constantly:** Stay invested through ups and downs
### Alternative for Higher Income
If you max 401(k) and IRA, open taxable brokerage account with similar index fund strategy.
keyPoints: Pay off high-interest debt (over 7% APR)- Have $1,000 starter emergency fund- Get employer 401(k) match if available

title: Introduction
content:
## Beyond Basic Investing
Once you've maxed traditional retirement accounts, sophisticated strategies can accelerate wealth building with tax advantages.
### Index Universal Life (IUL) Insurance
Permanent life insurance with cash value linked to stock index. Tax-free growth and access to funds. Best for high earners maximizing other accounts first. Requires careful product selection - many have high fees.
### Whole Life Insurance Banking
Build cash value while maintaining death benefit. Borrow against policy tax-free. Expensive in early years but can be valuable in comprehensive strategy. Not for everyone - requires long-term commitment.
### Tax-Loss Harvesting
Sell losing investments to offset gains and reduce taxes. Reinvest in similar assets. Advanced investors can save thousands annually in taxable accounts.
### When to Use Advanced Strategies
After maxing 401(k), IRA, HSA, and building taxable investments. Work with fee-only financial advisor to evaluate fit for your situation.

title: Introduction
content:
## Pay Less, Keep More
Tax-advantaged accounts are government incentives to save for specific goals. Using them strategically saves thousands over your lifetime.
### Retirement Accounts
- **401(k)/403(b):** Pre-tax contributions, tax-deferred growth- **Roth 401(k):** After-tax contributions, tax-free growth and withdrawals- **Traditional IRA:** May be tax-deductible, tax-deferred growth- **Roth IRA:** After-tax contributions, tax-free forever
### Health Savings Account (HSA)
Triple tax advantage: deductible contributions, tax-free growth, tax-free withdrawals for medical expenses. Best tax advantage available. Save receipts and let it grow for retirement healthcare costs.
### 529 College Savings
Tax-free growth and withdrawals for education expenses. Many states offer tax deductions for contributions.

title: Introduction
content:
## The Tax Planning Window
December is tax planning month. Actions taken before year-end can significantly impact your tax bill.
### Key December Strategies
- **Max retirement contributions:** Contribute to 401(k) before year-end- **Harvest tax losses:** Sell losing investments to offset gains- **Bunch charitable gifts:** Donate two years' worth in one year to exceed standard deduction- **Pay January expenses early:** Medical bills, property taxes if itemizing- **Review withholding:** Adjust W-4 to avoid large refund or bill
### January-April Actions
Contribute to IRA by April 15th for prior year. Gather documents and file early to get refund quickly. Consider professional help if return is complex.

title: Introduction
content:
## When You're Your Own Boss
Self-employed individuals face different tax rules and opportunities. Understanding them saves money and avoids penalties.
### Key Differences
- Pay self-employment tax (15.3% for Social Security/Medicare)- Make quarterly estimated payments to avoid penalties- Deduct business expenses directly- More complex record-keeping required
### Valuable Deductions
- Home office (if dedicated space)- Vehicle expenses (standard mileage or actual)- Equipment and supplies- Professional development- Health insurance premiums- SEP-IRA or Solo 401(k) contributions
### Retirement Advantages
SEP-IRA: Contribute up to 25% of income ($69,000 limit). Solo 401(k): Contribute up to $23,000 as employee plus 25% as employer. Much higher limits than regular jobs.
keyPoints: Pay self-employment tax (15.3% for Social Security/Medicare)- Make quarterly estimated payments to avoid penalties- Deduct business expenses directly- More complex record-keeping required
Home office (if dedicated space)- Vehicle expenses (standard mileage or actual)- Equipment and supplies- Professional development- Health insurance premiums- SEP-IRA or Solo 401(k) contributions

title: Introduction
content:
## Make Your Savings Work Harder
Traditional savings accounts pay nearly 0% interest. High-yield savings accounts can pay 4-5%, adding hundreds in free money annually.
### Key Benefits
- 4-5% APY vs 0.01% at big banks- FDIC insured up to $250,000- Quick access to funds (1-2 days)- No fees or minimums at top banks
### Top Options
Look for online banks like Ally, Marcus, or Discover. They offer higher rates because they have lower overhead than traditional banks.
### When to Use
Perfect for emergency funds and short-term savings goals (under 5 years). Not ideal for long-term wealth building - use investments instead.
keyPoints: 4-5% APY vs 0.01% at big banks- FDIC insured up to $250,000- Quick access to funds (1-2 days)- No fees or minimums at top banks

title: Introduction
content:
## Everything Is Negotiable
Most people overpay for services by never asking for better rates. Companies expect negotiation and have retention departments ready to offer discounts.
### Top Candidates for Negotiation
- Cable/internet (average savings: $30-50/month)- Cell phone plans ($15-30/month)- Insurance (auto, home)- Credit card interest rates- Medical bills- Gym memberships
### Negotiation Script
"I've been a customer for X years and I'm reviewing my expenses. I found a competitor offering similar service for $Y less. Can you match or beat that rate to keep my business?"
### The Power Move
Actually get competing quotes before calling. Be prepared to switch if they won't negotiate. Often they'll transfer you to retention with better offers.
keyPoints: Cable/internet (average savings: $30-50/month)- Cell phone plans ($15-30/month)- Insurance (auto, home)- Credit card interest rates- Medical bills- Gym memberships

title: Introduction
content:
## Flexibility Is Strength
Life changes, and goals should adapt. Rigid adherence to outdated goals wastes resources and creates frustration.
### When to Adjust Goals
- Major life changes (job, family, health)- Goal becomes unrealistic or irrelevant- Consistently missing targets by wide margin- Priorities shift based on new information- Unexpected opportunities arise
### Adjustment Strategies
**Extend timeline:** Keep goal but allow more time. **Reduce target:** Lower amount but maintain direction. **Pause temporarily:** Focus on urgent priorities. **Replace entirely:** Pursue more aligned goal.
### No Failure, Only Learning
Changing goals isn't failure - it's wisdom. Review what you've learned and apply insights to new goals.

title: Introduction
content:
## Reduce Your Tax Bill
Deductions reduce taxable income. Credits reduce taxes owed dollar-for-dollar. Credits are more valuable.
### Standard vs Itemized Deductions
Standard deduction (2025): $14,600 single, $29,200 married. Itemize only if total deductions exceed standard. Most people take standard deduction.
### Common Deductions
- Mortgage interest- State and local taxes ($10,000 limit)- Charitable contributions- Student loan interest (up to $2,500)- Medical expenses (over 7.5% AGI)
### Valuable Credits
- **Child Tax Credit:** Up to $2,000 per child- **Earned Income Credit:** For low-moderate income- **Saver's Credit:** For retirement contributions- **Education credits:** American Opportunity and Lifetime Learning
keyPoints: Mortgage interest- State and local taxes ($10,000 limit)- Charitable contributions- Student loan interest (up to $2,500)- Medical expenses (over 7.5% AGI)

title: Introduction
content:
## Ultimate Financial Security
A 6-month emergency fund provides incredible peace of mind and resilience against life's biggest challenges.
### Calculate Your Target
- List essential monthly expenses (housing, food, utilities, minimum debt payments)- Multiply by 6- Add 10% buffer for unexpected costs
### Timeline to Fully Funded
If saving $500/month toward a $15,000 goal: 30 months. Increase with raises, bonuses, and windfalls to accelerate.
### Milestones
- $1,000: Small emergency covered- 1 month: Major appliance replacement- 3 months: Short job loss survivable- 6 months: Complete security
### Where to Keep It
High-yield savings account earning 4-5%. Accessible but not in checking where it's tempting to spend.
keyPoints: List essential monthly expenses (housing, food, utilities, minimum debt payments)- Multiply by 6- Add 10% buffer for unexpected costs
$1,000: Small emergency covered- 1 month: Major appliance replacement- 3 months: Short job loss survivable- 6 months: Complete security

title: Introduction
content:
## The Set-It-And-Forget-It Strategy
Dollar-cost averaging means investing a fixed amount regularly regardless of market conditions. This removes emotion and timing risk.
### How It Works
Invest $500 monthly regardless of whether market is up or down. When prices are low, you buy more shares. When high, you buy fewer. Over time, this averages out to good results.
### Benefits
- Removes emotion from investing- Prevents market timing mistakes- Makes investing automatic- Works with any budget- Reduces impact of volatility
### Implementation
Set up automatic monthly investments from checking to investment account. Choose consistent day (like payday). Never try to time the market. Stay the course.
keyPoints: Removes emotion from investing- Prevents market timing mistakes- Makes investing automatic- Works with any budget- Reduces impact of volatility

4. When that's paid off, roll that payment to the next highest rate
5. Repeat until debt-free

Which categories are highest? Any surprises? Opportunities to reduce? Should budgets be adjusted?

title: What is the Debt Snowball?
content: A debt payoff strategy where you pay off debts from smallest to largest balance, regardless of interest rate.

title: How It Works
content:
1. List all debts from smallest to largest balance
2. Make minimum payments on all debts
3. Put extra money toward the smallest debt
4. When that's paid off, roll that payment to the next smallest
5. Repeat until debt-free
keyPoints: List all debts from smallest to largest balance
Make minimum payments on all debts
Put extra money toward the smallest debt
When that's paid off, roll that payment to the next smallest

content:

- Credit Card 2: $1,200 (minimum)
- Car Loan: $8,000 (minimum)
- Student Loan: $15,000 (minimum)

title: What is Budgeting?
content: A budget is a plan for how you'll spend your money each month. It helps you track income and expenses so you can reach your financial goals.

title: Why Budget?
content:
- Control your spending
- Save for important goals
- Reduce financial stress
- Avoid debt

title: Getting Started
content:
- Track your current spending for 30 days
- List all sources of income
- Categorize your expenses (housing, food, transportation, etc.)
- Set realistic spending limits for each category
keyPoints: Track your current spending for 30 days
Categorize your expenses (housing, food, transportation, etc.)

title: The 50/30/20 Rule
content: This simple budgeting method divides your after-tax income into three categories:

title: 50% - Needs
content:
- Housing (rent/mortgage)
- Utilities
- Groceries
- Transportation
- Insurance
- Minimum debt payments

title: 30% - Wants
content:
- Dining out
- Entertainment
- Shopping
- Hobbies
- Subscriptions

title: 20% - Savings & Extra Debt Payments
content:
- Emergency fund
- Retirement savings
- Extra debt payments
- Other savings goals

title: Example:
content:
If you earn $3,000/month after taxes:
- $1,500 for needs
- $900 for wants
- $600 for savings

title: Short-term (0-1 year)
content:
- Vacation
- Holiday gifts
- Home repairs

title: Mid-term (1-5 years)
content:
- Down payment
- Car purchase
- Wedding

title: Long-term (5+ years)
content:
- Retirement
- College fund
- Early retirement

title: SMART Goals Framework
content:
- Specific: Define exactly what you're saving for
- Measurable: Set a dollar amount
- Achievable: Make it realistic
- Relevant: Align with your values
- Time-bound: Set a deadline
keyPoints: Specific: Define exactly what you're saving for

Instead of "Save money for vacation"
Try: "Save $2,400 for Hawaii trip in 12 months ($200/month)"
